cards.text: To dynamically allocate an array of 10 integers: {{c1::int* array{ new int[10] };}}}
To delete a dynamically allocated array, use the {{c1::array}} form of delete: {{c2::delete[] array;}}

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.code:
int* array{ new int[10] };
delete array;
cards.answer: Using delete instead of delete[] for a dynamically allocated array causes undefined behavior. Should use delete[] array;
cards.explanation: Scalar delete only deletes the first element. Array delete (delete[]) properly deallocates the entire array.

cards.front: What is the difference between new and new[] operators?
cards.back:
new: allocates a single object/variable
new[]: allocates an array of objects

Must match with corresponding delete or delete[]

cards.explanation:
Dynamic arrays can be initialized using uniform initialization (C++11 and later):
  • Zero initialization: new int[10]{}
  • List initialization: new int[5]{1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
  • Partial initialization: new int[10]{1, 2} - remaining elements are zero-initialized
examples: int* array1{ new int[5]{} };        // all zeros
int* array2{ new int[3]{1, 2, 3} }; // initialized values

cards.text: Unlike fixed arrays, dynamic arrays can have their length determined at {{c1::runtime}}: {{c2::int* array{ new int[length] }; // length can be a variable}}

cards.front: Can you resize a dynamically allocated array after creation?
cards.back:
No, dynamic arrays cannot be resized. You must:
  • Allocate a new array of the desired size
  • Copy elements from old array to new array
  • Delete the old array
Use std::vector for automatic resizing.

cards.question: What happens if you forget delete[] for a dynamic array?
cards.answer: Memory leak - when the function returns, the pointer is destroyed but the 1000-element array remains allocated and cannot be freed.
cards.explanation: The dynamically allocated array persists after the pointer goes out of scope, creating a memory leak. Always match new[] with delete[].

cards.front: Do dynamically allocated arrays perform bounds checking?
cards.back: No. Like fixed arrays, dynamic arrays do NOT check if indices are valid. Accessing out of bounds causes undefined behavior.

cards.text: When passing a dynamically allocated array to a function, also pass the {{c1::length/size}} as a separate parameter because dynamic arrays don't know their own size.

cards.explanation:
std::vector provides:
  • Automatic memory management (no manual new/delete)
  • Built-in size tracking
  • Bounds checking with at()
  • Easy resizing
  • Exception safety
Use dynamic arrays only when interfacing with old C code or in performance-critical scenarios.

cards.front: What does sizeof return for a dynamically allocated array pointer?
cards.back: sizeof returns the size of the pointer itself (typically 4 or 8 bytes), NOT the size of the array. Dynamic arrays don't track their own size.

cards.question: What is the correct pattern for using a dynamic array?
cards.answer: This is correct: allocate with new[], use the array, then deallocate with delete[] and set to nullptr.
cards.explanation: Always match new[] with delete[], and set the pointer to nullptr after deletion to avoid dangling pointers.

cards.text:
Three ways to initialize a dynamic array of 5 ints:
Zero-init: {{c1::new int[5]{} }}
List-init: {{c2::new int[5]{1,2,3,4,5} }}
Partial: {{c3::new int[5]{1,2} }}
Always match allocation with deallocation: {{c1::new}} with {{c2::delete}}, {{c3::new[]}} with {{c4::delete[]}}
When passing a dynamic array to a function, you must also pass the {{c1::length/size}} because arrays don't know their own {{c2::size}}.
